items.q: Why are dried foods less prone to spoilage?
items.correct: Low water activity prevents microorganisms from growing.
items.misconception: Bacteria do not like dry textures.
items.why: Students think it's about texture preference. Microbes need available water. Drying reduces WATER ACTIVITY below the threshold microbes can use - no water = no metabolism = no spoilage.

items.correct: Energy used to maintain basic bodily functions at complete rest.
items.why: Students think BMR is about exercise. It's the baseline energy needed just to STAY ALIVE - breathing, heart, brain, organ function - typically 60-70% of total daily energy use even before any activity.

items.q: What is the difference between a food allergy and an intolerance?
items.correct: An allergy involves the immune system; intolerance does not.
items.misconception: They are the same with different severity.
items.random_wrong: Allergies are temporary, intolerances are permanent.
items.why: Students treat these as severity points on one spectrum. They have different mechanisms - allergies trigger immune (IgE) responses (potentially fatal anaphylaxis); intolerances are digestive (e.g., lactase deficiency).

items.q: The food safety 'danger zone' is approximately:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 5-63 °C. “0-5 °C” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: HACCP has how many core principles?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 7. “12” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If a bacterium doubles every 20 minutes, after 1 hour 1 cell becomes how many cells?
items.why: Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
